skill.name: pysam
skill.description: Guidance for using pysam to read, write, query, and summarize genomic SAM, BAM, CRAM, VCF, BCF, FASTA, and FASTQ files.
skill.summary: Use pysam for local genomic file parsing, region queries, coverage analysis, and sequencing workflow examples.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported critical and high findings for network access, Windows SAM credentials, weak cryptography, and command execution. Manual review found those severe alerts are false positives caused by genomics terminology, markdown examples, and pysam local fetch() calls. The remaining publication risk is medium because the skill legitimately documents local file writes and pysam wrappers around samtools and bcftools.

medium_findings.title: External Bioinformatics Command Wrappers
medium_findings.description: The skill documents pysam.samtools and pysam.bcftools wrappers for sorting, indexing, viewing, and converting genomic files. This is expected for a pysam skill and I found no shell-string construction or credential access, but generated workflows can execute local bioinformatics tools and create output files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines directly show pysam.samtools and pysam.bcftools calls. Confidence is high that external tool wrappers are present, while malicious intent is not shown.

medium_findings.title: Local Genomic File Read and Write Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill includes examples that open, read, write, convert, index, and summarize local genomic files. This is core pysam behavior, but users should review output paths because examples can overwrite or generate BAM, VCF, FASTQ, FASTA, and CSV files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples use local file handles and output writers. The risk is limited to expected filesystem effects in user-directed genomic workflows.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Windows SAM Credential Database Alerts
low_findings.description: The static scanner treated SAM as the Windows Security Accounts Manager database. In this skill, SAM means Sequence Alignment/Map, a standard genomics alignment format used with BAM and CRAM; no Windows credential database access was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context explicitly describes genomic alignment files. I found no evidence of Windows system file paths, registry access, or credential extraction.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Fetch Network Alerts
low_findings.description: The static scanner interpreted fetch() as a network Fetch API call. In these files, fetch() is a pysam method for retrieving local genomic regions from indexed alignment, sequence, variant, or tabix files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited sections define fetch() using genomic coordinates and local file indexes. No HTTP request construction or remote endpoint use appears in those contexts.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Markdown Backtick and Weak Crypto Alerts
low_findings.description: Many command-execution alerts came from markdown code fences and inline code formatting, not executable Ruby or shell backticks. Weak-crypto alerts did not map to cryptographic operations; the reviewed lines discuss genomic file modes, coordinates, CIGAR operations, and VCF header fields.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced contexts are documentation examples and genomics terminology. I did not find evidence of hashing, encryption, password handling, or dynamic command execution at these locations.

low_findings.title: External Documentation and Platform Links
low_findings.description: The skill includes a link to official pysam documentation and a suggestion to consider K-Dense Web for complex workflows. These are outbound references and promotional guidance, but I found no automatic network call, telemetry, or data exfiltration behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are visible documentation text rather than executable code. The confidence is lower because marketplace policy may still require review of promotional guidance.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Bioinformatics Command Wrappers
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents pysam.samtools and pysam.bcftools wrappers for sorting, indexing, viewing, and converting genomic files. This is expected for a pysam skill and I found no shell-string construction or credential access, but generated workflows can execute local bioinformatics tools and create output files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines directly show pysam.samtools and pysam.bcftools calls. Confidence is high that external tool wrappers are present, while malicious intent is not shown.

content.value_statement: Genomic sequencing work often requires careful handling of large local alignment, variant, and sequence files. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code produce pysam workflows for querying regions, calculating coverage, and writing filtered outputs.
actual_capabilities: Open and query SAM, BAM, and CRAM alignment files by genomic region.
Read, filter, and write VCF and BCF variant records with sample fields.
Extract reference sequences from indexed FASTA files using coordinate ranges.
Process FASTQ reads sequentially for quality, length, and format tasks.
Build coverage, pileup, read filtering, and variant support workflows.
Use pysam wrappers for common samtools and bcftools operations.
limitations: The skill is documentation and examples, not a bundled genomic dataset or runtime service.
Random access requires the correct BAM, CRAM, FASTA, VCF, or BCF index files.
Coordinate conventions differ across pysam APIs, region strings, and VCF records.
Generated workflows may read or write large local files and need path review.

use_cases.title: Inspect alignment evidence
use_cases.description: Query reads around genes or variants, summarize mapping quality, and inspect pileup evidence.

use_cases.title: Review variant files
use_cases.description: Filter VCF or BCF records, inspect genotype fields, and compare variants with coverage context.

use_cases.title: Build local file workflows
use_cases.description: Create repeatable scripts for indexing, converting, subsetting, and summarizing genomic files.

prompt_templates.title: Open a BAM region
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to use pysam to open a BAM file and list reads overlapping chr1 from 1000 to 2000.

prompt_templates.title: Filter variants by quality
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a pysam workflow that reads a VCF file, keeps high-quality variants, and reports chromosome, position, allele, and depth.

prompt_templates.title: Calculate coverage by interval
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a pysam coverage workflow for a BED list of target regions, including index checks and clear output summaries.

prompt_templates.title: Combine BAM, VCF, and FASTA
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a robust pysam plan to validate VCF variants with BAM read support and FASTA reference context while handling coordinate differences.

output_examples.input: Summarize alignment quality for a targeted BAM region.
output: Region summary with total reads, mapped reads, duplicate count, mean mapping quality, and coverage range.
A short note flags whether the BAM index is required for the requested region query.

output_examples.input: Review variants in a VCF file for a clinical gene panel.
output: A concise variant table with chromosome, position, reference allele, alternate allele, quality, depth, and genotype status.
A warning section explains any missing INFO or FORMAT fields.

output_examples.input: Extract FASTQ reads that pass quality and length filters.
output: A workflow outline that streams reads, keeps passing entries, writes a filtered file, and reports kept read counts.
A performance note recommends streaming for large compressed FASTQ files.

best_practices: Confirm input paths, output paths, and overwrite behavior before running generated workflows.
State the coordinate convention for each input and convert positions explicitly.
Use indexed files and streaming iterators to avoid loading large genomic datasets into memory.
anti_patterns: Assuming fetch() is a network request in pysam workflows.
Mixing 0-based pysam coordinates with 1-based VCF or region-string coordinates without conversion.
Writing filtered BAM, VCF, FASTQ, or CSV files without reviewing destination paths.

faq.question: Does SAM here mean the Windows credential database?
faq.answer: No. In this skill, SAM means Sequence Alignment/Map, a genomics file format for aligned sequencing reads.

faq.question: Does pysam fetch data from the internet?
faq.answer: No. The documented fetch() calls retrieve records from local indexed genomic files by region.

faq.question: Can this skill write files?
faq.answer: Yes. Many pysam workflows write local outputs such as BAM, VCF, FASTQ, FASTA, CSV, or index files.

faq.question: When are index files required?
faq.answer: Region queries usually require indexes such as BAI, CRAI, FAI, TBI, or CSI, depending on file type.

faq.question: Does this skill call samtools or bcftools?
faq.answer: It documents pysam wrappers for samtools and bcftools. Review generated commands and file paths before running them.

faq.question: What coordinate issue should users watch for?
faq.answer: Pysam numeric ranges are often 0-based and half-open, while VCF positions and some region strings are 1-based.
